title: Nature & Method: Marginal Analysis & Opportunity Cost
simple_html:
Managerial economics applies economic logic to business decisions. Its two foundational tools are marginal analysis and opportunity cost — together they answer the manager's everyday question: "Should I do a little more of this, or not?"

The core idea

Marginal means "one more unit." Instead of looking at totals or averages, marginal analysis compares the extra benefit of an action (marginal benefit, MB) with its extra cost (marginal cost, MC). The rule is simple: keep doing the activity as long as MB is greater than MC, and stop where MB = MC. That point gives the best (optimal) level of activity.

Opportunity cost

Opportunity cost is the value of the next-best alternative you give up when you make a choice. Economic decisions use opportunity cost, not just money actually spent. If you use a building you already own for your shop, the "free" building still has a cost: the rent you could have earned. Ignoring this leads to bad decisions.

Why it matters

Sunk costs (money already spent and unrecoverable) should be ignored in decisions — only future marginal costs and benefits count. This single insight prevents "throwing good money after bad."

Key takeaways

  • Decide at the margin: compare extra benefit vs extra cost.
  • Optimal level is where MB = MC.
  • Cost = opportunity cost (next-best alternative forgone), including implicit costs.
  • Ignore sunk costs; they cannot be changed by today's choice.
  • Economic profit (which subtracts opportunity cost) differs from accounting profit.
intermediate_html:
Managerial economics is the application of microeconomic theory and decision science to managerial problems — pricing, output, investment, and resource allocation. Two principles dominate: marginal analysis for finding optimal levels of activity, and opportunity cost for measuring the true cost of choices.

Marginal analysis

For any continuous decision (how much to produce, how many staff to hire, how much to advertise), define the objective as net benefit: NB = TB - TC. Net benefit is maximized where the slope of total benefit equals the slope of total cost — that is, where marginal benefit equals marginal cost:

MB = MC

If MB > MC, doing one more unit adds more than it costs, so expand. If MB < MC, contract. The optimum is the balance point. Note that you maximize net benefit, not total benefit — chasing maximum output or revenue usually overshoots the profitable level.

Opportunity cost and economic profit

Economists define cost as opportunity cost: the highest-valued alternative sacrificed. This splits into explicit costs (out-of-pocket payments to outsiders, e.g., wages, rent paid) and implicit costs (the forgone return on resources the owner already owns, e.g., the salary the owner could earn elsewhere, or interest on invested capital). Accounting profit subtracts only explicit costs; economic profit subtracts both:

Economic profit = Total revenue - Explicit costs - Implicit costs

A firm earning zero economic profit is doing exactly as well as its next-best alternative — this is called a normal profit, and it is a perfectly acceptable outcome.

Worked example

Maria quits a $70,000/year job to run a bakery. The bakery brings in $300,000 revenue. Explicit costs (ingredients, rent, wages) are $200,000. She also invested $50,000 of savings that would have earned $4,000 in interest.

  • Accounting profit = 300,000 - 200,000 = $100,000.
  • Implicit costs = 70,000 (forgone salary) + 4,000 (forgone interest) = 74,000.
  • Economic profit = 100,000 - 74,000 = $26,000.

The positive economic profit tells Maria the bakery beats her next-best alternatives by $26,000 — a genuinely good decision, which the accounting number alone could not confirm.

How to apply it

  1. Frame the decision as "a bit more vs a bit less," not "all or nothing."
  2. Identify the relevant marginal benefit and marginal cost — only amounts that change with the decision.
  3. Exclude sunk costs entirely; include implicit/opportunity costs that are real but unbilled.
  4. Set MB = MC to find the optimal scale.

Common pitfalls

Confusing average with marginal (a profitable average does not mean the next unit is profitable); counting sunk costs; and ignoring implicit costs, which makes mediocre ventures look profitable. This lesson sets up the rest of the course: demand and elasticity (Lesson 2) feed the marginal-benefit/revenue side, while production and cost theory (Lessons 4-5) build the marginal-cost side.
full_html:
Managerial economics is the discipline that bridges economic theory and managerial practice, applying microeconomic principles and tools of decision science to optimize choices a firm faces — pricing, output, input mix, investment, and risk. Spencer and Siegelman's classic definition frames it as "the integration of economic theory with business practice for the purpose of facilitating decision-making" (Spencer & Siegelman, 1964). At its methodological core sit two ideas introduced by the marginalist revolution of the 1870s (Jevons, 1871; Menger, 1871; Walras, 1874): marginal analysis and opportunity cost.

The marginalist method

Classical economics struggled with the diamond-water paradox: why is water, essential to life, nearly free, while diamonds, useless, are dear? The answer is marginal, not total, utility — value is set at the margin of consumption, where water is abundant (low marginal utility) and diamonds scarce (high marginal utility). The same logic governs production decisions. Any optimizing decision is solved by examining the change in the objective from a small change in the choice variable.

Formally, let net benefit be NB(Q) = TB(Q) - TC(Q). The first-order condition for an interior maximum is dNB/dQ = 0, i.e., MB(Q) = MC(Q), with the second-order condition that marginal benefit cut marginal cost from above (MB falling relative to MC). This equimarginal principle generalizes: when allocating a fixed budget across several uses, allocate so the marginal benefit per dollar is equal everywhere — otherwise you could reallocate from a low-MB/$ use to a high one and gain.

Opportunity cost: the economist's concept of cost

The notion that cost is forgone alternative was sharpened by the Austrian school (Wieser, 1889) and later by the London School of Economics tradition (Robbins, 1932; Buchanan, 1969). Robbins (1932) famously defined economics as "the science which studies human behaviour as a relationship between ends and scarce means which have alternative uses" — scarcity makes every choice a sacrifice of alternatives. Cost is therefore inherently subjective and forward-looking: it is the value of what is given up, evaluated at the moment of decision.

Operationally, this yields the explicit/implicit distinction. Explicit costs are contractual cash outlays; implicit costs are the opportunity costs of owner-supplied resources (the owner's time, owned premises, equity capital). Economic cost sums both, and so:

Economic profit = TR - explicit costs - implicit costs

Zero economic profit is a normal profit — the resources are earning exactly their opportunity cost. In competitive long-run equilibrium, free entry drives economic profit to zero (a result developed in Lesson 6, Market Structures), yet firms remain because accounting profit equals their normal return.

Sunk costs and relevant costs

A corollary is the irrelevance of sunk costs — expenditures already incurred and unrecoverable. Because opportunity cost is the value of alternatives now available, and a sunk outlay has no alternative use, it cannot enter a rational decision. The behavioral literature documents the sunk-cost fallacy (Arkes & Blumer, 1985; Thaler, 1980), in which decision-makers irrationally honor past investments — "we've already spent $2m, we can't stop now." Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory (1979) explains this via loss aversion: abandoning a project realizes a felt loss. Managerial discipline requires evaluating only incremental (forward-looking) cash flows, a principle that reappears in capital budgeting.

Worked example with marginal reasoning

A consultancy can take on additional client-hours. Revenue per hour is fixed at $400 (its marginal benefit). Marginal cost rises with fatigue and overtime: MC = $100 + $3 per hour worked that week. Optimal hours solve MB = MC: 400 = 100 + 3Q ⇒ Q = 100 hours. At Q = 90, MC = $370 < $400, so the 91st hour adds $30 of net benefit — expand. At Q = 110, MC = $430 > $400 — contract. Note the firm should ignore the $50,000 it spent last year on a training program (sunk) when setting this week's hours.

Now consider opportunity cost layered on top

Suppose those same consultants could instead be deployed on an internal product yielding $380/hour with certainty. Then the relevant marginal benefit of client work is its excess over the alternative, and the comparison shifts. This is exactly the equimarginal logic: deploy each hour where its marginal value is highest, equalizing marginal value across uses.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Average-marginal confusion. A product line with healthy average margin may have unprofitable marginal units; decisions must use marginal, not average, figures.
  • Maximizing the wrong thing. Maximizing revenue, market share, or output overshoots the profit-maximizing scale where MR = MC (a special case of MB = MC developed in Lessons 6-7).
  • Counting sunk costs / ignoring implicit costs. The first inflates costs of going forward; the second understates the true cost of capital and owner time, flattering weak ventures.
  • Treating cost as objective and historical. Cost is forward-looking opportunity cost, not the number in last year's ledger.

Connections across the syllabus

Marginal benefit operationalizes as marginal revenue once we model Demand & Elasticity (Lesson 2) and the optimum becomes the celebrated MR = MC rule across Market Structures (Lesson 6) and Pricing Practices (Lesson 7). Marginal cost is derived from Production Theory (Lesson 4) and Cost Theory (Lesson 5). Opportunity cost underlies Consumer Theory (Lesson 3, the budget constraint) and reappears in Game Theory (Lesson 8) as the payoff comparison. Mastering marginal thinking and opportunity cost is thus the prerequisite for everything that follows.
